8  The increasing demand for high reliability in modern en-
8 gineered systems, driven by the need for product quality and
6 cost efficiency, has made fault detection and isolation (FDI)
9
crucial. Unexpected failures, particularly in safety-critical
1
. and high-precision applications, can cause severe operational
4
disruptions and substantial  economic  losses. To mitigate
0
these risks, advanced FDI methods are essential  for early
5
2  fault  detection,  preventing catastrophic  failures,  lowering
:
maintenance costs, and minimizing unplanned downtime. By
v
i  leveraging FDI techniques, engineered systems can operate
X with greater efficiency and safety,  ensuring uninterrupted
r
production and long-term sustainability [1], [2].
a
Model-based FDI methods have been an active area of
research over the past two decades [3]–[8]. These methods
rely on accurate system models  to characterize deviating
behavior of measured system data from the behaviour of
such models.  In such appraoaches,  first,  system models
are identified using input-output data and, second, model-
based fault diagnosis methods are designed [9]–[11]. While

is with Proven Guarantees using
urrent Neural Networks
3  1
Ferrari Trecate , and Nathan van de Wouw
model-based methods are reliable and provide performance
guarantees, in practice, obtaining accurate system models is
challenging,  time-consuming, and often impossible due to
the high complexity of the system under study.
Therefore, recent research has explored data-driven meth-
ods that bypass the need for explicit system identification,
instead leveraging the foundational connection between sys-
tem identification and model-based fault  diagnosis  tech-
niques [12], [13]. These data-driven approaches simplify FDI
schemes by omitting the explicit system identification step
while still achieving comparable performance to model-based
methods. Hereafter, we refer to such data-driven techniques
as direct methods.
The development of direct methods is still ongoing, even
for  linear  systems.  For  instance,  [14]  and [15] propose
system inversion-based methods, which reconstruct unknown
inputs or disturbances by reversing system dynamics — an
approach that enables fault estimation without requiring an
explicit system model. These inversion-based methods have
also been extended to nonlinear systems [16]. Another class
of direct methods for nonlinear systems are projection-based
schemes, which map the system dynamics  into a higher-
dimensional space to establish a linear representation [17],
[18]. This allows the use of linear data-driven techniques for
the design of fault detection and isolation (FDI) schemes.
Given the flexibility of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) in
learning complex high-dimensional functions, they have been
used to learn direct mappings from input-output data to un-
known fault signatures [2], [19], [20]. While neural network-
based methods demonstrate high accuracy,  they often lack
theoretical performance guarantees for fault diagnosis. The
latter observation motivates the development of DNNs-based
FDI methods for nonlinear systems with provable diagnostic
guarantees that enable the isolation of single, multiple, and
simultaneous additive faults. In this paper, we will develop
such a method for sensor faults.
Leveraging recent results in [21], we exploit Robust Re-
current Neural Networks  (R-RENs),  a class of  recurrent
neural networks with a direct parameterization that inherently
guarantees the satisfaction of input-output incremental IQCs
(Integral Quadratic Constraints), without requiring additional
constraints during training. The use of incremental IQC prop-
erties provides provable diagnostic guarantees. This property,
when applied to the construction of a bank of fault detectors,
ensures that fault detector filters remain insensitive to faults
for which they are not specifically designed. Furthermore,
the direct parameterization of R-RENs simplifies learning,
enabling the use of standard optimization techniques such as

isolation for nonlinear discrete-time systems is described in
Section II. The proposed solution is outlined in Section III.
The results of  this approach are evaluated via numerical
results  in Section  IV. The final  remarks,  together with
recommendations for future work, are provided in Section V.

II. PROBLEM FORMULATION
Consider the discrete-time nonlinear system:
(cid:40) +
x = g(x, u),
(1)
y = h(x, u) + f,
Rn  Rm  Rl
where x ∈  , y ∈  ,  and u ∈  are the system
state, measured output and known input, respectively, with
N  Rn  Rl  Rn
n,m, l ∈  . Functions g  :  ×  →  and h  :
Rn  Rl  Rm
×  →  describe the system dynamics and output
Z  Rm
mapping,  respectively. Signal f  :  →  denotes  the
unknown sensor fault vector. We assume no model for system
(1) is available. We seek to design a scheme to detect and
isolate fault occurrences in individual or multiple entries of
the measured output  (i.e.,  faults in one or more sensors),
given input-output (u, y) data only.
Inspired by model-based FDI methods, we propose an
isolation scheme based on a bank of filters. Each filter aims
to detect a fault in a particular sensor while robustifying the
impact of faults in all  the others. The i-th filter generates
a residual ri,  i = 1, . . . ,m, which serves as an indicator
for fault occurrence in the i-th sensor. These residuals are
then used by a decision-making algorithm to determine the
system status (healthy/faulty) and identify the faulty sensor
(if the system is faulty). Here, the focus of this work is on the
design of the filter bank, not the decision-making process.
Without loss of generality, the structure of each filter can
be characterized as follows:
(cid:40) +
zi = ai(zi, u¯; θi),  i = 1, . . . ,m,
(2)
ri = bi(zi, u¯; θi),
Rnz
where zi  ∈  represents  the  internal filter  state,  and
the scalar residual ri  is the output of the filter. We collect
inputs and outputs in the stacked vector u¯ := col[u, y] ∈ U¯,
¯  Rl+m
for  some compact  set U ⊂  . The filter  functions
ai(·) and bi(·),  and parameters θi, i = 1, . . . ,m,  are  to
be designed. Here, we aim to design well-posed filters that
converge (independent of their initial condition) to a unique,
asymptotically stable steady-state solution, associated to the
bounded filter input u¯ ∈ U¯. Such property guarantees that
despite the nonlinearity of the filter, each measured input-
output pair (∈ u¯) will  lead (after a transient) to a unique
residual signature in the output of the filter. These properties
are effectively represented by the concept of convergence
(see [22]–[24]). In the following definition, we introduce the
notion of convergence for the FD filters in (2).
Definition 1 (Convergence) The fault detector filters (2) are
said to be globally exponentially convergent for a class of
bounded inputs U¯,  if,  for every u¯ ∈ U¯, and for all  i =
1, . . . ,m:
• there exists a unique and bounded steady-state solution
z¯i(k, u¯) to (2);
• z¯i(k, u¯) is globally exponentially stable.
Given the fact that no model of system (1) is available to
design the filters, we pursue a data-driven approach for which

We pursue a deep learning approach to the design of the
fault detection filters. The design process involves solving
a training problem that minimizes a cost function evaluated
over the synthetic dataset (fault-free input-output trajectories
and synthetic fault signals) and the filter structure.
Recent  results  in [21]  introduce  the notion of Robust
acyclic Recurrent Equilibrium Networks (R-aRENs). Using
such Robust acyclic RENs (R-aRENs) and by the grace of

their contractivity we guarantee the first goal (i.e., conver-
gence of each filter) in Problem 1. Moreover, using the built-
in satisfaction of input-output incremental IQCs for the R-
aRENs, we address the third and fourth properties we aim
to achieve in Problem 1. More specifically, the incremental
IQC properties of  the R-aRENs, with a specific selection
of design parameters (provided later), can exhibit an entry-
2
based incremental ℓ -gain from a filter’s input to its output.
Using this entry-based gain, for each fault detector filter, we
can tune a high gain for the fault entry for which the filter is
designed, leading to a necessary condition for fault sensitivity
(i.e., the third goal in Problem 1). Using the same gain, we
can tune a low gain for the other entries (other than the fault
entry),  leading to a sufficient condition for insensitivity to
other faults (i.e., the last goal in Problem 1).
Furthermore,  note  that  the properties of R-aRENs are
built-in behavioral guarantees. That  is,  to ensure the IQC
properties, no parametric constraints needs to be imposed
during the learning of R-aRENs. This simplifies the learning
process because it allows the use of general methods for
unconstrained optimization, such as, e.g., stochastic gradient
descent and its variations [21]. Furthermore, as discussed
in the introduction, RENs demonstrate high flexibility by
covering different types of nonlinear systems [21].
Considering the general structure of the FD filters in (2),
we select ai(·) and bi(·) such that the FD filters are in the
form of R-aRENs as provided in [21]. Therefore,  the FD
filters can be written as

In  [21, Thm.  1],  an Linear Matrix  Inequality  (LMI)
condition is provided to ensure contractivity and incremental
Integral Quadratic Constraints (IQC) to induce a robustness
property for R-aRENs. This LMI condition can also be
used to satisfy some of  the requirements  in Problem 1.
This means that during the learning process of FD filters,
a semi-definite program with the LMI condition should be
solved to ensure the aforementioned properties. Imposing the

In this section, we evaluate the proposed method through
a nonlinear case study. The case study is a four-degree-of-
freedom roll plane maneuver of a vehicle with nonlinear
suspension [25],  [26]. A schematic representation of  this
system is provided in Figure 1. The equations of motion and
system parameters are detailed in Appendix C. The system’s
nonlinearity arises from the nonlinear stiffness and damping
between the roll bar and both tires. The available measured
outputs include the relative displacement and relative velocity
between  the  roll  bar  and both  tires. The system inputs
correspond  to  the  road surface positions  (u1  and u2  in
Figure 1).
For  the aforementioned system,  all  four  sensors may
become faulty,  and the objective is  to design a bank of
four filters using the proposed REN-based approach in Sec-
tion III. We assume that input-output data from the system
are available,  categorized into training and test data-sets,
which are generated by applying different system inputs, as

Roll bar
Tires
Road surfaces
Fig. 1.  Roll plane system schematic.
outlined in Section IV-A. Synthetic fault data are generated
and added to the sensor measurements. Notably,  even in
experimental settings, no actual faulty data are required, as
they are synthesized. The process of generating synthetic
fault data is also discussed in Section IV-A. Following the
proposed method, each R-aREN fault detector filter is trained
using the optimization problem in (5). The training problem
(5)  is implemented in PyTorch, and the R-aRENs (which
can be seen as deep neural networks) are  trained using
ADAM, a stochastic gradient descent method. The code to
reproduce this example is available at https://github.
com/DecodEPFL/faultDiag.

various system inputs. These inputs consist of combinations
of sinusoidal signals at different frequency ranges to encom-
pass a broad spectrum of input variations. The system input,

the first sensor.
To simplify the training process, we have only considered
faults in the first and second sensors. We have trained the
model using five sets of data for  the healthy case, five
for each individual faulty sensor, and five for each of the
simultaneous faulty sensors (i.e., simultaneous fault  in the
first and second sensors). This results in in 20 sets of training
data (i.e.,  sf = 20 in (5)), which are drawn from the
previously described system input and fault distributions.
To evaluate the results, we present one independent  test
dataset drawn from the same distributions. However,  the
filter’s response for additional test data can be found in the
link provided above to the code. Since the studied system has
continuous-time dynamics, we have discretized it using the
fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) method at 100 Hz, and it is
simulated for 20 seconds. For  training the fault detection
filters, however,  the data are downsampled to 4 Hz (i.e.,
kf = 80 in (5)).
B. Performance of Fault Detector Filters
The residual signals (i.e.,  the outputs of  the FD filters)
for all four FD filters, corresponding to a test case where
a fault occurs in the first sensor, are depicted in Figure 2.
Each plot corresponds  to a filter designed for a specific
sensor, as indicated in the titles. As shown, after the fault
occurs in the first sensor, its corresponding residual exhibits
a clear signal, allowing for fault detection despite the small
magnitude of  the fault.

Furthermore,  since the filters are
trained to follow the fault signal, the residual generators can
serve as fault estimators due to their high accuracy. However,
fault estimation is not the focus of this work.
We have also evaluated the performance and reliability
of  the designed fault detectors for fault occurrence in the
first sensor,  the second sensor, and simultaneous faults in
both the first and second sensors over the entire set of 1000
test data sets. To assess performance, the mean Root Mean
Square Error (RMSE) over the 1000 data sets is computed.

This work has presented a model-free Fault Detection
and Isolation (FDI) methodology for discrete-time nonlinear
systems, capable of diagnosing single, multiple, and simul-
taneous sensor faults. Leveraging historical healthy input-
output  data,  the proposed approach has utilized a bank
of Robust Recurrent Neural Networks  (R-RENs), where
each filter is designed to be specifically sensitive to faults
in a particular sensor while remaining robust  to faults in
others. A key advantage of this method is its performance
guarantees in terms of the convergence property (providing
robustness) and the sensitivity (and insensitivity) to faults.
These performance guarantees are inherently imposed by
the filter structure.This allows for efficient  learning using
standard unconstrained optimization techniques. Numerical
simulations on a roll-plane model of a vehicle have demon-
strated the effectiveness of  the proposed methodology for
sensor fault detection and isolation. Future work could in-
clude providing a direct parameterization for R-REN fault
detectors to ensure a sufficient condition for fault sensitivity,
as discussed after Problem 1. Moreover, it may include the
extension of this framework to address more complex fault
scenarios, including process and actuator faults.

A. Direct Parameterization of R-aRENs
Here, we present the direct parameterization of a R-aREN
which can be applied to all  the FD filters for the sensors.
For the sake of notational simplicity, we omit the subscript
i that indicates each FD filter.

B. Proof of Theorem 1
Firstly, note that an R-aREN-based FD filter is well-posed
if a unique solution is guaranteed. It is shown in [21, Sec.
III.B] that  if the direct parameterization in Appendix A is
imposed on the filters (3), then the R-aREN-based filters are
well-posed.
Next, we prove the convergence property for the FD filters
(3) as R-aRENs. Then, we establish the necessary condition
for the sensitivity to faults and subsequently demonstrate the
sufficient condition for the insensitivity to other faults.
1. Convergence: Let  us first  introduce  the  following
lemma, which is used to ensure the convergence property
for the FD filters (3).
Lemma 1  [22, Thm. 3] Consider the general structure of
FD filters (2).  If  there exist a symmetric positive definite
Rnz×nz

Furthermore, due to the boundedness of u¯, w, and ηz, we

can infer that a(0, u¯) is bounded, satisfying (12) for the FD
filters. Therefore, we can conclude the convergence property
of the FD filters (3) using Lemma 1.
2. Fault Sensitivity Condition: Since the direct parame-
terization in Appendix A is imposed on the filters (3), it is
shown in [21] that the following IQC property [21, Def. 3]
holds for the filters (3), which are of type R-aREN:

Now, consider the healthy case (i.e., where f is zero in (1)) as
scenario s1 and the occurrence of a fault in the i-th sensor as
scenario s2. In these two scenarios, the input-output data are
identical except for the i-th entry of the sensor measurement,
yi (due to the introduction of the fault fi in the i-th sensor).
Therefore, the only non-zero incremental term related to the
system input-output  is the third term in the left-hand side
of (15). Moreover,  the term on the right-hand side of (15)
is zero because we have the same initial conditions ci = di
for these two scenarios. Therefore, we can conclude that the
condition (6) holds.
3. Other Faults Insensitivity Condition: Similar to the
above results, the incremental inequality (15) can be applied
between the healthy scenario s1 and a fault occurrence in
any sensor except  the i-th sensor, denoted as scenario s3.
In these scenarios, the input-output data are identical except

for certain entries of sensor measurements yj where j ̸= i
(caused by the fault vector f = f˜ with at least one non-zero
entry at j ̸= i and zero entry at i). Therefore, the only non-
zero incremental term related to the system input-output is
the fourth term on the left-hand side of (15). Additionally,
similar to the previous result, the term on the right-hand side
of (15) is zero because we have the same initial conditions
ci = di  for  these  two scenarios. Consequently, we can
conclude that the condition (7) holds.
In conclusion, all three properties of the FD filters of the
type of R-aRENs in (3) have been proven.
